questions.question: Explain where reinforcement learning fits within the broader machine learning taxonomy. How does it differ from supervised and unsupervised learning paradigms?
keyPoints: Reinforcement learning is one of three main ML paradigms (alongside supervised and unsupervised)
RL learns through agent-environment interaction unlike supervised/unsupervised which use static datasets
RL uses delayed reward feedback while supervised has immediate labels and unsupervised has no feedback
RL involves sequential decision-making where actions affect future states
RL has exploration-exploitation trade-off not present in other paradigms

questions.question: What is the exploration-exploitation trade-off in reinforcement learning? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Exploration: trying new actions to discover potentially better rewards
Exploitation: using known actions that yield good rewards
Must balance both to maximize long-term returns
Too much exploration wastes opportunities; too much exploitation misses better solutions

questions.question: List and briefly explain the core components of the reinforcement learning framework.
keyPoints: Agent: The learner/decision-maker
Environment: System the agent interacts with
State: Current situation representation
Action: Decisions the agent can make
Reward: Immediate feedback signal
Policy: Strategy for choosing actions
Value Function: Long-term return estimate
Model (optional): Environment behavior

questions.question: Under what circumstances would you choose reinforcement learning over supervised learning for solving a problem?
keyPoints: When no labeled dataset exists but environment is available for interaction
When problem involves sequential decisions with interdependent choices
When rewards are delayed (consequences not immediately apparent)
When optimal behavior must be learned through trial and error
When dynamic adaptation to environment is required
